entry.title: DevSecOps
entry.content:
---
title: DevSecOps Explained for Beginners
description: Learn DevSecOps from basics to lifecycle with real examples.
---

# 🔐 DevSecOps Explained for Beginners

> 🚀 A simple guide to understanding DevSecOps and why it matters

---

## 🎨 Slide Version (Canva)

👉 View full presentation here:  
🔗 https://www.canva.com/design/DAG_uCMdKUU/sgVgyobe71QhR5-EtSNutg/edit?utm_content=DAG_uCMdKUU&utm_campaign=designshare&utm_medium=link2&utm_source=sharebutton

---

## 🧩 What is DevSecOps?

DevSecOps stands for:

👉 **Dev + Sec + Ops**

- **Dev (Development):** Writing code  
- **Sec (Security):** Protecting systems  
- **Ops (Operations):** Running and maintaining infrastructure  

💡 The key idea:

> All teams work **together, continuously, and automatically**

---

## 🔄 DevSecOps Lifecycle

A typical DevSecOps workflow includes:

1. Collaboration & Communication  
2. Threat Modeling  
3. QA Integration  
4. Security Checks & Scans  
5. Vulnerability Management  
6. CI/CD  
7. Infrastructure as Code (IaC)  
8. Container Security  
9. Key Management  
10. Continuous Monitoring  

---

## 🤝 1. Collaboration & Communication

### Why it matters

Teams (Dev – Sec – Ops) should:

- Work together  
- Share information continuously  

### Benefits

- 🔥 Early bug detection  
- 🔥 Fewer conflicts  
- 🔥 Faster development  
- 🔥 Stronger security  

---

## 🧠 2. Threat Modeling

### What is it?

Thinking like a hacker before building the system:

> “If I were an attacker, where would I attack?”

### Key questions:

- What does the system include?  
- What are the critical assets?  
- What threats exist?  
- How can we prevent them?  

💡 Fixing issues early = cheaper and easier  

---

## 🧪 3. QA Integration

### Traditional approach ❌

1. Code  
2. Deploy  
3. Test at the end  

👉 Result:
- Many bugs  
- Delays  
- Stress  

---

### DevSecOps approach ✅

- Test continuously  
- Automate testing  

👉 Result:
- Bugs found early  
- Faster releases  

---

## 🔍 4. Security Checks & Scans

### SAST
- Scan source code  
- Find vulnerabilities before running  

### DAST
- Test running applications  
- Simulate real-world attacks  

🎯 Goal:
> Detect vulnerabilities as early as possible  

---

## 🛡️ 5. Vulnerability Management

Security is not just about finding bugs — it's about managing them.

### Process:

1. Scan  
2. Assess severity  
3. Prioritize  
4. Fix continuously  

👉 Focus on critical issues first  

---

## ⚙️ 6. CI/CD

CI/CD automates the development pipeline:

- ✅ Build automatically  
- ✅ Test automatically  
- ✅ Deploy automatically  
- ✅ Integrate security checks  

💡 Every code push triggers automation  

---

## 🏗️ 7. Infrastructure as Code (IaC)

Infrastructure is defined using code.

### Benefits:

- Easy to manage  
- Consistent environments  
- Reduced human error  

---

## ⚡ Why DevSecOps?

### Old way ❌

- Teams work separately  
- Security comes late  

👉 Result:
- Late bug detection  
- Expensive fixes  

---

### DevSecOps way ✅

- Teams collaborate  
- Security is built-in  

👉 Result:
- Faster delivery  
- Better security  
- Higher efficiency  

---

## 🧠 Final Thoughts

DevSecOps is not just a process —  
👉 it is a **mindset**

> Build fast, but secure from the beginning.

---

## 📌 Summary

- DevSecOps = Dev + Sec + Ops  
- Focus on **automation + collaboration + security early**  
- Essential skill for modern developers  

---

🔥 Start your DevSecOps journey today!

entry.title: Blockchain & Web3 – Guide from Basics to Practice
entry.summary: Explaining Blockchain, Web3, Smart Contracts, and Web3 Wallets in an Easy-to-Understand Way for Beginners
entry.content:
📌 Introduction​

Blockchain and Web3 are core technologies of the new generation of the Internet.
If Web2 helps users interact and create content, then Web3 aims for a bigger goal:
👉 giving users ownership of data and digital assets.

This article is designed for beginners, helping you:

  • Understand what Blockchain is
  • Understand how Web3 differs from Web2
  • Know how Smart Contracts work
  • Get an overview before diving deeper

📊 Illustrative slide for the entire lesson:
👉 Blockchain & Web3 – Canva

1️⃣ What is Blockchain?​

Blockchain (blockchain) is a distributed database, where data is:

  • Recorded into Blocks
  • Blocks are linked together through encryption
  • Data that has been recorded cannot be modified

🔗 Key Features of Blockchain​

  • Decentralized: No central server
  • Transparent: Anyone can verify transactions
  • Immutable: Recorded data is almost impossible to delete

📌 Example:

When you transfer money on the blockchain, that transaction will be recorded permanently and everyone can check it.

2️⃣ Basic Structure of Blockchain & Web3​

A Web3 ecosystem usually consists of the following components:

🔹 Block​

  • Contains transaction data
  • Has a hash of the previous block

🔹 Hash​

  • Is an encrypted string representing the data
  • Changing just 1 character → hash changes completely

🔹 Node​

  • Computers participating in the blockchain network
  • Each node stores a copy of the data

🔹 Wallet (Web3 Wallet)​

  • Represents the digital identity of the user
  • Stores private key
  • Examples: MetaMask, Trust Wallet

📊 Illustrative slide for the structure section:
👉 View on Canva

3️⃣ What is a Smart Contract?​

A Smart Contract is:

A program that runs automatically on the blockchain, without intermediaries.

🧠 Features​

  • Automatically executes
  • Cannot be edited after deployment
  • Public & transparent

✍️ Simple Smart Contract Example (Solidity)​

pragma solidity ^0.8.0;

contract HelloWeb3 {
    function hello() public pure returns(string memory) {
        return "Hello Web3!";
    }
}

entry.title: Guide to Creating a Blog with Docusaurus
entry.summary: 1. What is a Blog? What is Docusaurus?
entry.content:
1. What is a Blog? What is Docusaurus?​

Blog is a platform for sharing knowledge and documenting the learning process.
Docusaurus is a framework for quickly building blog/documentation websites, built on React.

2. Tools Used​

  • NodeJS
  • VS Code
  • Docusaurus
  • GitHub Pages

3. Blog Building Process​

The slide below describes the entire process of building a website,
from initializing the project to deploying the website on GitHub Pages.

👉 View the detailed guide slide here:
🔗 https://www.canva.com/design/DAG-x-HN2J0/AXmxIe_VDDip6aalEPP8PQ/view

(The slide will open in a new tab for a full and clear view)

4. Conclusion​

After viewing the slide, newcomers can:

  • Create a Docusaurus project
  • Write blog posts
  • Push source code to GitHub
  • Deploy the website using GitHub Pages

entry.title: Understanding Java Platform – Execution Flow & JVM Architecture
entry.content:
1. Java Platform Overview​

Java is not just a programming language, but a complete platform (Java Platform).

👉 When we say "programming in Java", we are actually working with 3 core components:

  • Java Language
  • JVM (Java Virtual Machine)
  • Ecosystem of libraries & tools

Core Objectives of Java​

Write Once, Run Anywhere (WORA)

Write code once, run it on:

  • Windows
  • Linux
  • macOS
    … without modifying the code

2. Roles of JDK, JRE, JVM​

Many Java learners confuse these 3 concepts, so they need to be clarified absolutely.

2.1 JVM – Java Virtual Machine​

What is JVM?

  • JVM is a virtual machine
  • Runs Java bytecode
  • Acts as an intermediary between Java and the operating system

📌 JVM:

  • Does not understand .java
  • Does not understand .exe
  • Only understands .class (bytecode)

👉 Each operating system has its own JVM, but:

  • Bytecode is the same

2.2 JRE – Java Runtime Environment​

JRE includes:

  • JVM
  • Core Java Libraries (java.lang, java.util, ...)
  • Runtime configuration files

👉 JRE is used to:

  • Run Java programs
  • ❌ Not for writing or compiling code

📌 If the machine only has JRE:

  • Can run .class
  • ❌ Cannot compile .java

2.3 JDK – Java Development Kit​

JDK = JRE + Development Tools

Includes:

  • javac (compiler)
  • java (launcher)
  • javadoc, jdb, ...

👉 JDK is used to:

  • Write code
  • Compile
  • Run
  • Debug

📌 When installing Java for programming: ➡️ ALWAYS need JDK

2.4 Overall Relationship​

JDK
├── JRE
│ ├── JVM
│ └── Java Core Libraries
└── Development Tools

3. Relationship between Java Language and Java Platform​

Java Language​

  • Syntax and keywords
  • Data types
  • OOP (class, object, inheritance)
  • Source code files .java

Java Platform​

  • JVM (Java Virtual Machine)
  • ClassLoader Subsystem
  • Runtime Data Areas
  • Execution Engine & Garbage Collector

Java Language is just the surface
Java Platform is what determines how the program runs

4. Java Execution Flow​

When you run a Java program, JVM does not execute the .java file directly.

General Flow​

IDE
↓ (Save)
Hello.java (Hard Drive)
↓ (Compile)
javac Hello.java
↓
Hello.class (Bytecode)
↓
JVM (RAM)
↓
OS
↓
CPU Execution

5. Compile-time and Runtime in Java​

5.1 Compile-time​

Occurs when running:

javac Hello.java
